teaching_arc.entry_point: Open by connecting to Session 2: the computer is ready, so now students need to understand the ROS system that is running on it.
teaching_arc.progression: Review sourcing and workspaces, define packages, executables, and nodes, build the ROS graph mental model, compare topics, services, actions, and parameters, then practice by creating, running, inspecting, recording, and debugging a tiny ROS system.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to inspect a ROS 2 system in order: environment, workspace, package, executable, node, interface, topic/service/action, graph, bag, and error message.

audience_level: undergraduate robotics lab students who have just prepared ROS 2 Humble and now need a practical mental model for packages, nodes, graph inspection, and debugging
topic_goal: Teach students to see ROS 2 as a graph of running programs and communication interfaces, then use that mental model to build and debug a small system.
topic_takeaways: Students should know that a package organizes code, while a node is a running process.
Students should distinguish topics, services, actions, parameters, and bags by when they are used.
Students should know how to inspect a ROS graph with CLI tools and rqt_graph before guessing.
Students should leave with a debugging order they can reuse when manipulator labs become larger.
style_notes: Keep reminding students that command memorization is less important than knowing what question each command answers.
During the in-class exercise, sound like a live instructor: pause before expected results, explain why each file matters, and connect mistakes to the debugging ladder.
Treat rqt_graph and ros2 bag as observation tools, not decorations.
Close by handing forward to robot description, frames, TF, and RViz visualization.
transition_style: Move from setup recall to graph concepts, then from concepts to a small runnable system, and finally from that system to a repeatable debugging order.

slide_ref.source_title: Session 3 - ROS 2 Graph, Packages, Nodes, and Debugging

slides.narration_seed: Open by saying that Session 2 made the machine ready. Session 3 makes the ROS system understandable.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students understand ros2 graph packages nodes debugging in a way they can explain, apply, and build on later.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why This Session Matters so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Session 3 - ROS 2 Graph, Packages, Nodes, and Debugging as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Session 3 - ROS 2 Graph, Packages, Nodes, and Debugging connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Session 3 - ROS 2 Graph, Packages, Nodes, and Debugging sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Tell students that the point is not memorizing every command. The point is knowing where to look first.
must_cover: ROS 2 is the wiring between robot state, visualization, simulation, and planning.
Manipulator systems are multi-node systems; no single file controls everything.
Debugging starts with observation: what is running, what is publishing, and what is missing?
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What You Should Be Able To Do Today so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.story_hint: Keep Why This Session Matters connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Why This Session Matters clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: What You Should Be Able To Do Today

slides.narration_seed: This slide tells students the practical target. It also gives you the lecture map.
must_cover: Read the ROS graph with ros2 commands and rqt_graph.
Create a Python package and know where node code belongs.
Build and source your workspace without guessing.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Why This Session Matters into What You Should Be Able To Do Today so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROS 2 In A Manipulator Workflow so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Keep this as a preview. The focus today is the graph and the tools, not MoveIt configuration.
must_cover: Robot model describes links, joints, and frames.
ROS graph connects state publishers, controllers, visualization, and planners.
RViz2 shows robot state, TF, markers, and trajectories.

slides.narration_seed: Use this as the first live check. If many students fail here, pause and fix setup habits before moving on.
must_cover: Expected ROS distro: humble.
Expected workspace habit: build from ~/ros2_ws, then source the overlay.
If a command fails: check .bashrc, source order, and current folder first.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Quick Setup Check to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Quick Setup Check is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep Quick Setup Check connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Quick Setup Check accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: This slide answers why source is not optional. New terminals do not automatically know ROS paths unless .bashrc sets them.
must_cover: Sourcing teaches the terminal where ROS is installed.
Without sourcing, commands such as ros2 may not exist in that terminal.
For this course, the base source file is /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: The next slide should compress the lesson into a quick memory or self-check.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Why Sourcing Matters first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why Sourcing Matters connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Why Sourcing Matters clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Use the underlay/overlay vocabulary, but translate it immediately: base ROS, then your own packages.
must_cover: Underlay: /opt/ros/humble gives the base ROS installation.
Overlay: ~/ros2_ws/install gives your local class packages.
Source order matters: base first, workspace second.
Students should be able to explain the core idea behind ros2 graph packages nodes debugging.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Workspace Recap to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Underlay First, Overlay Second so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Workspace Recap to compress the lesson into memorable statements, not to reread the slide.
slides.story_hint: Keep Workspace Recap connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Workspace Recap with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: This reinforces that the environment is layered. A fresh terminal is a simple reset when students source things in a confusing order.
must_cover: Underlay first: source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash gives the base ROS packages.
Overlay second: source ~/ros2_ws/install/setup.bash adds your local packages.
Overlay can override underlay when package names overlap, so keep names organized.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The ROS Development Loop so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Underlay First, Overlay Second first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Underlay First, Overlay Second connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Underlay First, Overlay Second clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Emphasize that build and source are separate steps. Many errors come from skipping source after a build.
must_cover: Edit files inside ~/ros2_ws/src/ .
Build from ~/ros2_ws using colcon build --symlink-install.
Source ~/ros2_ws/install/setup.bash so the terminal sees new output.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What Does colcon build Actually Do? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.story_hint: Keep The ROS Development Loop connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.

slide_ref.source_title: What Does colcon build Actually Do?

slides.narration_seed: This answers the student's natural question: why do we build if Python is not compiled like C++?
must_cover: Find packages: it looks inside src/ for packages and dependencies.
Prepare output: it creates or updates build/, install/, and log/.
Install entry points: it makes Python executables available through ros2 run.
Generate environment files: it creates install/setup.bash for your overlay.
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep What Does colcon build Actually Do? efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on What Does colcon build Actually Do? and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: This is not deep build theory. It gives students practical commands they can use immediately.
must_cover: --symlink-install is helpful for Python-heavy beginner work.
--packages-select rebuilds one package when you know what changed.
--executor sequential can help weaker laptops build one package at a time.
If the terminal gets weird, open a new one and source again.
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Build Habits That Reduce Confusion efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Build Habits That Reduce Confusion and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: This is one of the most important distinctions for beginners. Repeat it using one concrete package example.
must_cover: Package = code, metadata, dependencies, launch files, and config files.
Executable = a runnable entry point installed from the package.
Node = the executable while it is running inside ROS.
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Build Habits That Reduce Confusion into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Package vs Executable vs Node explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Keep Package vs Executable vs Node connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Package vs Executable vs Node and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Tell students that many package errors are simply files placed in the wrong folder.
must_cover: Package root has package.xml, setup.py, resource/, and the inner Python module folder.
package.xml lists package metadata and dependencies.
setup.py tells Python what to install and what executables to expose.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Create A Class Package so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Keep the package name manipulator-flavored but the code simple. This is a ROS basics exercise, not arm control yet.
must_cover: ament_python means this is a Python ROS package.
rclpy is the Python client library for ROS 2.
std_msgs gives simple message types such as String.
ROS 2 package tutorial
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: Say clearly: package is on disk, executable is something you run, node is the running process.
must_cover: Node = running program that performs one clear job.
Examples: state publisher, controller, planner, camera driver, talker, listener.
A node can publish, subscribe, offer services, call services, or run actions.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into How Nodes Communicate so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for What Is A Node? first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep What Is A Node? connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture What Is A Node? clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Use the rule of thumb: stream -> topic, ask -> service, long task -> action.
must_cover: Topics are data streams: publish and subscribe.
Services are quick request-response calls.
Actions are long-running goals with feedback, result, and cancel.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The ros2 CLI Is A Map, Not A Memory Test so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for How Nodes Communicate first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep How Nodes Communicate connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture How Nodes Communicate clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slide_ref.source_title: The ros2 CLI Is A Map, Not A Memory Test

slides.narration_seed: This slide helps students see the command line as organized families instead of random commands.
must_cover: Start with the object you want to inspect: package, node, topic, service, action, interface, parameter, or bag.
Then choose the verb: list, info, echo, show, call, send_goal, record, play.
Use --help when you forget the exact pattern.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Interfaces: The Data Contract so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for The ros2 CLI Is A Map, Not A Memory Test first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep The ros2 CLI Is A Map, Not A Memory Test connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture The ros2 CLI Is A Map, Not A Memory Test clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slides.narration_seed: This is where students learn not to guess YAML payloads or message fields.
must_cover: Message (.msg): one data structure for a topic.
Service (.srv): request fields, then response fields.
Action (.action): goal fields, feedback fields, and result fields.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Interfaces: The Data Contract to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Interfaces: The Data Contract is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Interfaces: The Data Contract in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Interfaces: The Data Contract connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Interfaces: The Data Contract accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: The key idea is decoupling: publishers and subscribers do not need to know each other directly.
must_cover: Topic = named channel such as /chatter, /joint_states, or /tf.
Publisher sends messages to the topic.
Subscriber receives messages from the topic.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Topics Can Fan Out so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Topics: Streams Of Data first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Topics: Streams Of Data connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Topics: Streams Of Data clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: This is a gentle warning for later: if two nodes publish the same command topic, the robot may seem unpredictable.
must_cover: One topic can connect many nodes at the same time.
Multiple subscribers can listen to the same robot state stream.
Multiple publishers are possible, but they can confuse debugging if you did not expect them.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Topic CLI Commands so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Topics Can Fan Out first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Mention that topic names are live; if no node is running, some topics will not exist.
must_cover: list shows what topics currently exist.
info shows publisher/subscriber counts and the message type.
echo prints messages live.

slide_ref.source_title: Manipulator Topics You Will See Later

slides.narration_seed: This connects topics to robot arms without teaching TF or MoveIt deeply yet.
must_cover: /joint_states carries measured or simulated joint positions and velocities.
/tf carries changing frame transforms.
/tf_static carries fixed frame transforms.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Manipulator Topics You Will See Later to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.narration_seed: Explain circles and arrows using the screenshot. This is the fastest way to reduce guessing.
must_cover: Graph view shows nodes and topic connections visually.
Use it early when a system does not behave as expected.
Answer the first question: is the publisher or subscriber even running?

slides.narration_seed: This is useful in class because students often think their graph is wrong when it is just filtered or changing.
must_cover: Hidden items can be filtered out to keep the graph readable.
CLI tools can appear as nodes when they publish, echo, or inspect data.
A changing graph is normal because the graph shows what is alive right now.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The Graph Grows As You Run Tools so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slide_ref.source_title: The Graph Grows As You Run Tools

slides.narration_seed: Use this to explain why running a debugging command can itself change the graph.
must_cover: When you run a publisher, the graph shows a new node and topic connection.
When you run ros2 topic echo, the echo command can become another subscriber node.
This is good news: the tools are part of the observable ROS system.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Classroom Note: Strange Cross-Talk so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture The Graph Grows As You Run Tools clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: Make this optional. It is a troubleshooting tool, not a default forever setting.
must_cover: ROS 2 can discover other ROS systems on the same network.
In a classroom, another laptop may accidentally use the same topic names.
If I ask for isolation, use export ROS_LOCALHOST_ONLY=1 in that terminal.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Services: Quick Request And Response so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Use the mental model of asking a yes/no or compute-this-now question.
must_cover: Service = ask once, get one response.
Client sends a request.
Server returns a response.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Services: Quick Request And Response clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: Students may not have /clear unless turtlesim runs. Say commands depend on the nodes currently running.
must_cover: list shows available service names.
type reveals the service interface.
interface show shows request and response fields.

slides.narration_seed: This is the communication pattern students will recognize later in planning and execution workflows.
must_cover: Action = goal, feedback, result, and cancel.
Client sends a goal to an action server.
Server reports progress while working.
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

info shows type and client/server counts.
send_goal sends a long-running request.

slide_ref.source_title: Why Actions Matter For Robot Arms

slides.narration_seed: Keep this conceptual. No MoveIt install or project details here.
must_cover: Planning takes time: a planner may search, fail, retry, or return a trajectory.
Execution takes time: the robot follows a trajectory over several seconds.
Feedback matters: the system can report progress while the task is running.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Why Actions Matter For Robot Arms to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: Where This Is Going: Planned Motion

slides.narration_seed: This is the motivational manipulator slide. Keep it short and connect it back to ROS graph skills.
must_cover: The same graph ideas appear in MoveIt: nodes, topics, parameters, services, and actions.
A planned path is data that can be visualized, inspected, and later executed.
RViz2 is the window where we will inspect robot state and trajectories.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Parameters: Settings Inside Nodes so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: This slide prepares them for launch files, YAML, Gazebo time, and MoveIt configs.
must_cover: Parameter = named value stored inside a node.
Good for settings such as use_sim_time, frame names, gains, speeds, and file paths.
Not for sensor streams; use topics for data that changes continuously.

slides.narration_seed: Explain that /node_name is a placeholder. They must replace it with a real node name.
must_cover: use_sim_time is important when a system uses simulated time.
dump can save current parameters into a YAML-style snapshot.
Runtime changes may fail if the node does not allow that parameter change.

slides.narration_seed: Do not teach full launch syntax today. The point is why launch files exist.
must_cover: Launch file = repeatable startup recipe.
Starts multiple nodes with one command.
Sets parameters and remaps names so nodes connect correctly.
Keeps demos reproducible because everyone starts the same system.

slides.narration_seed: This is extremely useful in labs. It turns a one-time failure into data students can inspect.
must_cover: rosbag records topic messages into a file on disk.
Replay lets you debug later without rerunning the robot or simulator.
Good for evidence: save what the system actually published.

info summarizes what was recorded.

Timing problems: compare message frequency and timestamps.

must_cover: Step 1: create the manipulator_basics package.
Step 2: add talker.py and listener.py in the inner Python folder.
Step 3: edit setup.py so ros2 run can find both nodes.
Step 4: build, source, run, inspect, and record.

slides.narration_seed: This is the exact file placement that beginners often miss.
must_cover: Inner folder rule: the path repeats the package name once.
Correct code path: manipulator_basics/manipulator_basics/talker.py.
Save in nano with Ctrl+O, press Enter, then exit with Ctrl+X.
Do not put node files directly beside setup.py.

slide_ref.source_title: Common Errors And What They Mean

slides.narration_seed: Ask students to map each error to the debugging ladder.
must_cover: ros2: command not found -> base ROS was not sourced.
Package not found -> wrong workspace, not built, or overlay not sourced.
Executable not found -> entry point missing or package not rebuilt.

slides.narration_seed: Close by saying the next session will use this foundation for robot description, frames, TF, and RViz visualization.
must_cover: A robot application is a graph of nodes, topics, services, actions, and parameters.
A package is not a node; packages organize code, nodes run code.
Topics stream data; services answer quick requests; actions run long goals.
The debugging ladder from today will be reused when robot description, TF, RViz, and MoveIt make the graph larger.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What To Remember to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Bridge To The Next Session so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use What To Remember as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep What To Remember connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave What To Remember with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: This is not a project slide. It simply connects the ROS graph foundation to the next technical topic.
must_cover: Next topic: robot description, URDF/Xacro, TF, and RViz2.
Why today's work matters: robot models publish topics and transforms through the same graph.
What to keep practicing: ros2 node, ros2 topic, ros2 interface, rqt_graph, and ros2 bag.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by saying that the next session builds on this graph view with robot description, frames, TF, and RViz visualization.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Bridge To The Next Session first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Bridge To The Next Session connected to the lab sequence: the ROS graph is the foundation under robot descriptions, transforms, RViz, and eventually MoveIt.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave with closure for this session and a clear reason to care about robot description and TF next.
